case_name: Australian Property Group Pte Ltd v H.A. & Chung Partnership and others

audit_suggestion.case_name: Australian Property Group Pte Ltd v H.A. & Chung Partnership and others

label_change.evidence: The judgment concludes: 'I grant judgment to the plaintiff against the defendants as follows...' including monetary orders in APG's favour.
label_change.recommended_change: Replace the non-compliant label with the substantive plaintiff outcome label 'Claim Allowed'.

fact_changes.current_value: APG earned revenue from commissions paid by Australian property developers and incurred expenses including rent, staff costs and marketing costs.
fact_changes.recommended_value: APG was in the business of marketing and selling Australian real estate on behalf of Australian property developers, and its revenue came from commissions paid by property developers while its expenses included rent, staff costs and marketing costs.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 3 states APG was in the business of marketing and selling Australian real estate on behalf of Australian property developers, and that its revenue was the commission paid by the property developers whilst its expenses were related to rent, staff costs and costs incurred in marketing the real estate.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Rephrase to match the source precisely and keep only neutral background business facts.

fact_changes.current_value: On incorporation, APG issued fully paid-up ordinary shares as follows: 360,000 shares to Sean Colville Niven, 160,000 shares to Wang Zijian (James Wang), 40,000 shares to Gan Siong Teck, 200,000 shares to Hou Junyu and 40,000 shares to Lee Jer Ren, although the consideration for these shares had not in fact been paid.
fact_changes.recommended_value: On incorporation, APG had the following directors and shareholders: Sean Colville Niven (360,000 ordinary shares), Wang Zijian (160,000 ordinary shares), Gan Siong Teck (40,000 ordinary shares), Hou Junyu (200,000 ordinary shares) and Lee Jer Ren (40,000 ordinary shares). The shares were issued as fully paid-up, but in fact had not been paid for.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 6 states APG had the following directors and shareholders and separately notes that although the shares were issued as fully paid-up, in fact they had not been paid for.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Separate the director/shareholder list from the unpaid consideration point and avoid implying the shares were 'issued' as fully paid-up in the same clause.

fact_changes.current_value: On incorporation, APG appointed Sean Colville Niven, Wang Zijian (James Wang), Gan Siong Teck, Hou Junyu and Lee Jer Ren as directors.
fact_changes.recommended_value: On incorporation, APG had the following directors and shareholders: Sean Colville Niven, Wang Zijian, Gan Siong Teck, Hou Junyu and Lee Jer Ren.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 6 states these persons were the directors and shareholders on incorporation; it does not say APG 'appointed' them in the wording used in the row.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Use the source's wording and avoid adding an unsupported appointment formulation.

fact_changes.current_value: Following the 11 March 2009 loan agreement, APG treated Gan Siong Teck, Hou Junyu and Lee Jer Ren as non-executive directors whose role included protecting the interests of lender Goh Cheng Liang, while Sean Colville Niven and Wang Zijian controlled APG's day-to-day operations.
fact_changes.recommended_value: Mr Gan, Mr Hou and Mr Lee acted as non-executive directors of APG to protect Mr Goh's interests while the defendants controlled the day-to-day operations of APG.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 7 states that Mr Gan, Mr Hou and Mr Lee acted as non-executive directors to protect Mr Goh's interests while the defendants controlled the day-to-day operations of APG.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Replace the inaccurate 'APG treated' wording with the source's direct statement.

fact_changes.current_value: In January 2010, Lim Kay-Lin Kathlene purchased 240,000 APG shares from Sean Colville Niven for $160,000.
fact_changes.recommended_value: In January 2010, she became a shareholder of the company when she purchased 240,000 shares from Mr Niven for $160,000.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 10 states that in January 2010, she became a shareholder when she purchased 240,000 shares from Mr Niven for $160,000.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Use the source's wording and avoid adding 'APG' before shares where the judgment does not.

fact_changes.current_value: APG engaged chartered accountant John Kennedy Stuart to provide tax services and advice to APG's clients.
fact_changes.recommended_value: APG had engaged John Kennedy Stuart, a chartered accountant, to provide tax services and advice to APG's clients.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 11 states APG had engaged John Kennedy Stuart to provide tax services and advice to APG's clients.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Remove the unsupported 'chartered accountant' insertion if not tied to the source wording in the row.

fact_changes.current_value: After being engaged for tax services, Lim Kay-Lin Kathlene asked John Kennedy Stuart to investigate and report on APG's financial situation.
fact_changes.recommended_value: Subsequently, Ms Lim also asked Mr Stuart to investigate and report on APG's financial situation.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 11 states that subsequently, Ms Lim also asked Mr Stuart to investigate and report on APG's financial situation.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Use the source's wording and avoid the unsupported 'after being engaged for tax services' phrasing.

fact_changes.current_value: In July 2010, Lim Kay-Lin Kathlene injected $25,000 into APG to help fund a marketing event.
fact_changes.recommended_value: Ms Lim had to put in a cash injection of $25,000 in July 2010 to help the company fund a marketing event.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 76 states Ms Lim had to put in a cash injection of $25,000 in July 2010 to help the company fund a marketing event.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Use the source's wording and avoid over-specific attribution if not necessary.

fact_changes.current_value: On 24 June 2010, APG received a demand from its advertising agency to pay an outstanding amount of $59,502.92 by 4 July 2010, with a subsequent notice on 16 July 2010 that legal action would be taken if payment was not received by 19 July 2010.
fact_changes.recommended_value: On 24 June 2010, APG received a demand from its advertising agency to pay an outstanding amount of $59,502.92 by 4 July 2010. On 16 July 2010, the agency informed APG that if payment was not received by Monday, 19 July 2010, legal action would be taken against it.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 77 gives these two separate events and dates.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Split into two sentences and preserve the exact dates and wording from the source.

fact_changes.current_value: Under the 6 September 2010 agreement, Sean Colville Niven acknowledged owing APG $300,450.37 and it was recorded that APG would write off 40% of this amount, with the remaining $180,270.22 to be paid by him in two instalments by 31 December 2011 and 31 December 2012.
fact_changes.recommended_value: Under the September Agreement, Mr Niven acknowledged that he owed the company $300,450.37 and it was agreed that the company would write off 40% of this amount with Mr Niven paying the balance $180,270.22 in two instalments: 50% by 31 December 2011 and the remaining 50% by 31 December 2012.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 12 states the debt amount, the 40% write-off, the balance of $180,270.22, and the two instalment dates.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Use the source's exact structure and avoid changing the agreement language to 'recorded that'.

fact_changes.current_value: The 6 September 2010 agreement recorded that unpaid share capital liabilities were allocated as $560,000 for Sean Colville Niven, $160,000 for Wang Zijian and $80,000 for Lim Kay-Lin Kathlene, and that shareholders would repay 10% of the outstanding capital each year for ten years.
fact_changes.recommended_value: The September Agreement provided for the payment of the unpaid share capital. The parties agreed that Mr Niven was liable for $560,000, Mr Wang was liable for $160,000 and Ms Lim for $80,000. By cl 1.3, the shareholders had to repay the company 10% of the outstanding capital each year for ten years.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 12 states these terms in this form.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Use the source's wording and avoid implying a mere allocation rather than an agreement on liability.

fact_changes.current_value: Under the 13 October 2010 document, it was recorded that Sean Colville Niven and Wang Zijian would resign as directors that day and transfer all their APG shares, except for 10% retained by Wang Zijian, to Lim Kay-Lin Kathlene, and that Lim Kay-Lin Kathlene would pay Sean Colville Niven's outstanding expenses and commission in instalments.
fact_changes.recommended_value: The October Agreement provided that Mr Niven and Mr Wang would resign as directors that very day and transfer all their shares, except for 10% in Mr Wang's hands, to Ms Lim. Ms Lim agreed to pay Mr Niven's outstanding expenses and commission in instalments.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 15 states these terms.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Use the source's wording and avoid adding 'APG' before shares.

fact_changes.current_value: The 13 October 2010 document recorded that upon settlement, Sean Colville Niven and Wang Zijian would have no further compliance-related liabilities to APG and would owe nothing to APG except for personal tax liabilities.
fact_changes.recommended_value: It was further agreed that Mr Niven and Mr Wang would have no liability to APG 'from a compliance perspective upon settlement' and that they 'will owe nothing to the company upon settlement, excluding any personal tax liabilities'.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 15 contains this exact wording.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Quote the source language more closely and avoid paraphrasing into 'compliance-related liabilities'.

fact_changes.current_value: On or about 17 November 2009, three OCBC cheques were drawn by Sean Colville Niven: cheque number 771893 for $320,000 payable to Lim Kay-Lin Kathlene, cheque number 771397 for $300,000 payable to APG, and cheque number 771399 dated 1 December 2009 for $490,000 payable to APG.
fact_changes.recommended_value: The defendants appear to be referring to three cheques drawn by Mr Niven, viz, OCBC cheque No 771893 dated 17 November 2009 for the sum of $320,000 to Ms Lim, OCBC cheque No 771397 also dated 17 November 2009 for the sum of $300,000 to APG and OCBC cheque No 771399 dated 1 December 2009 for the sum of $490,000 to APG. There are no cheques dated 17 December 2009.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 42 states the cheque numbers, dates and payees, and expressly notes there are no cheques dated 17 December 2009.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Correct the date and payee wording to match the judgment exactly.

fact_changes.current_value: Around 1 December 2009, part of the proceeds of an A$450,000 loan from Ubertas was deposited into Sean Colville Niven's personal account in Australia and then transmitted in Singapore dollars to APG through cheques including the $490,000 and $300,000 cheques.
fact_changes.recommended_value: At least A$450,000 of the $490,000 cheque was actually the company's own money, being the loan proceeds from the Ubertas loan. Mr Niven agreed that part of the Ubertas loan had been deposited into his own account in Australia and he had then made payment in Singapore dollars to APG.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraphs 44 and 45 state that at least A$450,000 of the $490,000 cheque was the company's own money and that Mr Niven agreed part of the Ubertas loan had been deposited into his own account and then paid to APG.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Remove the unsupported 'around 1 December 2009' and the implication that the $300,000 cheque was part of the same transmission.

fact_changes.current_value: On 22 February 2010, APG issued an invoice to Ubertas 350 William Street Pty Ltd for A$86,000 described as marketing funds for a property marketing campaign, with instructions on the invoice for payment to be made to Sean Colville Niven's personal bank account in Australia.
fact_changes.recommended_value: On 22 February 2010, APG raised an invoice to Ubertas 350 William Street Pty Ltd for the sum of A$86,000 to cover the marketing funds for the campaign. Mr Niven signed the invoice and inserted instructions on it for the funds to be paid to his personal bank account in Australia.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 36 states these facts.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Use the source's wording and avoid adding 'issued' if the judgment says 'raised'.

fact_changes.current_value: Following the 22 February 2010 invoice, Ubertas 350 William Street Pty Ltd paid A$86,000 into Sean Colville Niven's personal bank account in Australia.
fact_changes.recommended_value: On 22 February 2010, APG raised an invoice to Ubertas 350 William Street Pty Ltd for A$86,000 and Mr Niven inserted instructions for the funds to be paid to his personal bank account in Australia.
fact_changes.evidence: The payment into Mr Niven's account is described in the judgment as a consequence of the invoice and is discussed in the court's reasoning; for a pre-dispute factual row, the safer neutral fact is the invoice and instruction itself.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Remove the later-confirmed payment outcome and keep only the pre-dispute invoice/instruction fact.

fact_changes.current_value: On 6 February 2009, a joint current account with United Overseas Bank Ltd, account number 339xxx, was opened in the joint names of Sean Colville Niven and Wang Zijian.
fact_changes.recommended_value: In February 2009, the defendants had set up a joint current account with UOB Bank Ltd, Account No 339xxx, the defendants' joint account.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 90 states that in February 2009 the defendants had set up a joint current account with UOB Bank Ltd, Account No 339xxx.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Use the month-only timing from the source and the bank name as stated there.

fact_changes.current_value: Between 6 February 2009 and 31 May 2009, funds were regularly transferred from APG's bank account into the joint UOB account held by Sean Colville Niven and Wang Zijian.
fact_changes.recommended_value: In February 2009, the defendants had set up a joint current account with UOB Bank Ltd, Account No 339xxx.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 90 describes the joint account and states that copies of monthly bank statements for 6 February 2009 to 31 May 2009 were adduced in evidence; the transfer pattern is trial evidence and should not be retained for a pre-dispute row.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Remove the transfer pattern because it is trial-evidence material and not needed as neutral background.

fact_changes.current_value: On 8 December 2009, APG's board, then comprising Sean Colville Niven, Wang Zijian and Lim Kay-Lin Kathlene, passed a resolution allowing cheques drawn on APG's bank account to be signed by a single signatory, either Sean Colville Niven or Lim Kay-Lin Kathlene.
fact_changes.recommended_value: On 8 December 2009, Ms Lim was appointed as a director of APG.
fact_changes.evidence: The single-signatory resolution is discussed in the judgment as evidence at paragraph 91, which is trial-stage material; the neutral background fact in paragraph 10 is Ms Lim's appointment as director on 8 December 2009.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Replace the litigation-evidence resolution with the neutral appointment fact.

fact_changes.current_value: In November 2010, Lim Kay-Lin Kathlene repaid the outstanding Ubertas loan and obtained an assignment of APG's debt to Ubertas, thereby becoming APG's creditor in place of Ubertas.
fact_changes.recommended_value: In November 2010, Ms Lim repaid the Ubertas loan and obtained an assignment of the outstanding amount.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 72 states that in November 2010 Ms Lim repaid the Ubertas loan and obtained an assignment of the outstanding amount; the fuller creditor-substitution characterization is part of the court's later reasoning.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Trim to the neutral pre-dispute fact and remove the outcome-framing creditor substitution language.

fact_changes.current_value: From May 2010, Wang Zijian was based in Malaysia and was no longer involved in APG's daily operations and management matters.
fact_changes.recommended_value: From May 2010, Mr Wang had been in Malaysia and had no longer been involved in the daily operations or management matters of APG.
fact_changes.evidence: Paragraph 87 states this in Mr Wang's submissions; it is litigation-stage material and should not be retained in a pre-dispute factual row.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Remove this litigation-stage assertion.

fact_changes.current_value: On 1 March 2011, Lim Kay-Lin Kathlene applied to place APG under judicial management.
fact_changes.evidence: This is a litigation filing and falls after the temporal wall.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Remove the judicial management application from the pre-dispute factual row.

fact_changes.current_value: On 21 March 2011, APG was placed under judicial management and Timothy James Reid of Ferrier Hodgson Pte Ltd was appointed as judicial manager.
fact_changes.evidence: This is a procedural outcome and judicial-management appointment, which is after the temporal wall.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Remove the judicial management appointment from the pre-dispute factual row.

fact_changes.current_value: In July 2011, APG, acting through its judicial manager, commenced this action to recover money paid to the defendants.
fact_changes.evidence: This is the commencement of legal proceedings and is beyond the temporal wall.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Remove the litigation commencement fact.

facts_to_remove.current_value: On 2 July 2010, John Kennedy Stuart sent an email to APG's directors stating that, based on APG's cash flow difficulties, existing and pending financial commitments and expected receipts, APG was technically insolvent as at that date.
facts_to_remove.evidence: This email is quoted and relied on in the court's reasoning; it is trial evidence and judgment-stage material.
facts_to_remove.recommended_change: Remove trial evidence and judicially relied-upon insolvency analysis from the pre-dispute row.

facts_to_remove.current_value: On 2 July 2010, after receiving John Kennedy Stuart's email, Sean Colville Niven replied by email stating that the creditors report and revenue report had been visible to the shareholders for at least six months and that there was nothing new in the summary of APG's cash flow and creditors' position.
facts_to_remove.evidence: This is trial evidence quoted in the judgment.

facts_to_remove.current_value: On 6 October 2010, by written directors' resolution signed by Sean Colville Niven and Lim Kay-Lin Kathlene, APG approved the appointment of Ferrier Hodgson Pte Ltd to conduct a review of APG's accounts.
facts_to_remove.evidence: This is a board resolution discussed in the judgment and used as evidence.

facts_to_remove.current_value: The judgment found that APG's funds had been used for Sean Colville Niven's personal benefit, including American Express card bills, personal legal fees, undocumented payments, family travel expenses, payments to third parties for personal items, and the A$86,000 received from Ubertas 350 William Street Pty Ltd.
facts_to_remove.evidence: This is the court's finding and summary of judgment-stage conclusions.
facts_to_remove.recommended_change: Remove judicial findings and outcome-revealing allegations.

facts_to_remove.current_value: The court treated appropriation of company funds for personal use as a prima facie abuse of funds and applied authorities such as Creanovate Pte Ltd v Firstlink Energy Pte Ltd.
facts_to_remove.evidence: This is court reasoning and legal analysis.
facts_to_remove.recommended_change: Remove legal reasoning from the factual row.

facts_to_remove.current_value: The judge rejected the defendants' contentions that an Account Practice and an Oral Agreement authorised these payments, finding no contemporaneous vouchers or corroborating evidence and noting inconsistencies with clause 5 of the Goh loan agreement and the conduct of the non-executive directors.
facts_to_remove.evidence: This is judgment-stage reasoning and findings.
facts_to_remove.recommended_change: Remove judicial findings and evidentiary evaluation.

facts_to_remove.current_value: The court concluded that the alleged oral arrangements were not established.
facts_to_remove.evidence: This is a judicial conclusion.

facts_to_remove.current_value: The judge relied on the 2 July 2010 email from John Kennedy Stuart describing APG as technically insolvent, Sean Colville Niven's email responses acknowledging the creditors and revenue reports, evidence of rent arrears, creditor demands from the advertising agency, and the need for cash injections.
facts_to_remove.evidence: This is court reasoning and synthesis of trial evidence.
facts_to_remove.recommended_change: Remove judicial reasoning and evidence synthesis.

facts_to_remove.current_value: The court held that APG was insolvent or near insolvency by September and October 2010.
facts_to_remove.evidence: This is a judicial finding.

facts_to_remove.current_value: Applying principles from Chip Thye Enterprises Pte Ltd (in liquidation) v Phay Gi Mo and Liquidators of Progen Engineering Pte Ltd v Progen Holdings Ltd, the judge held that when a company is insolvent or in a parlous financial position, directors must consider creditors' interests and avoid transactions that dissipate assets to the prejudice of creditors.
facts_to_remove.evidence: This is legal reasoning and authority application.
facts_to_remove.recommended_change: Remove legal analysis from the factual row.

facts_to_remove.current_value: The judge examined the September and October Agreements and found that they required APG to write off a substantial portion of Sean Colville Niven's debt, assume half of his personal debt to the non-executive directors, rearrange and postpone payment of unpaid share capital over ten years, and effectively release the defendants from liabilities to APG without corresponding benefit to APG or its creditors.
facts_to_remove.evidence: This is judgment-stage analysis of the agreements.
facts_to_remove.recommended_change: Remove judicial findings about the agreements.

facts_to_remove.current_value: The court held that these agreements were not in the interests of APG's creditors and that the directors, including Lim Kay-Lin Kathlene, acted in breach of their duties in entering into them.
facts_to_remove.evidence: This is a judicial conclusion.

facts_to_remove.current_value: The agreements were treated as voidable and set aside, so they did not provide a defence to APG's claims or a basis for the counterclaims.
facts_to_remove.evidence: This is the judgment outcome on the agreements and counterclaims.

facts_to_remove.current_value: Regarding unpaid share capital, the judge found that neither Sean Colville Niven nor Wang Zijian had paid for their original share allotments.
facts_to_remove.evidence: This is a judicial finding.

facts_to_remove.current_value: The court rejected the argument that certain payments in November and December 2009 constituted share capital payments, noting that they largely represented transmission of Ubertas loan funds, were not pleaded as capital payments, and were inconsistent with the September Agreement in which Sean Colville Niven accepted liability for unpaid capital of $560,000.
facts_to_remove.evidence: This is judicial reasoning and evaluation of evidence.
facts_to_remove.recommended_change: Remove the court's evaluation of the alleged capital payments.

facts_to_remove.current_value: The court held that Sean Colville Niven remained liable for unpaid capital on the shares he continued to hold (400,000 shares, including 40,000 acquired from the non-executive directors) but not for the 240,000 shares transferred to Lim Kay-Lin Kathlene before the call, as liability for calls follows the registered holder at the time of the call under APG's articles.
facts_to_remove.evidence: This is a judicial conclusion on liability.

facts_to_remove.current_value: Wang Zijian was held liable for $160,000 in unpaid capital on his 160,000 shares.
facts_to_remove.evidence: This is the court's outcome on liability.

facts_to_remove.current_value: The judge rejected the argument that whatever benefited the 'rain-maker' was necessarily in APG's interest and applied an objective test of whether an honest and diligent person could reasonably regard the transactions as beneficial to the company.
facts_to_remove.evidence: This is legal reasoning and judicial analysis.

facts_to_remove.current_value: The court found that many expenses were clearly personal, lacked supporting documentation, or were not shown to be incurred for APG's business.
facts_to_remove.evidence: This is a judicial finding on the evidence.

facts_to_remove.current_value: The judge held that Sean Colville Niven was liable to repay the sums claimed, subject to deduction of amounts already recovered from the first defendant.
facts_to_remove.evidence: This is the judgment outcome.

facts_to_remove.current_value: The judge distinguished between Wang Zijian's lack of personal benefit from the impugned payments and his responsibilities as a director.
facts_to_remove.evidence: This is judicial reasoning.

facts_to_remove.current_value: The judge found that he did not control APG's cheque books after the single-signatory resolution and that he was not directly responsible for cheques drawn solely by Sean Colville Niven.
facts_to_remove.evidence: This is a judicial finding based on evidence.

facts_to_remove.current_value: However, because funds were transferred from APG's account into the joint UOB account held by both defendants, the court held that both defendants had to account jointly and severally for sums paid into that joint account and to repay any amounts not shown to have been used for APG's purposes.
facts_to_remove.evidence: This is the court's outcome on the accounting claim.

facts_to_remove.current_value: The court declined to treat general allegations about accounting systems as a separate pleaded claim for damages.
facts_to_remove.evidence: This is judicial reasoning and a procedural ruling.

facts_to_remove.current_value: The judge did not make findings on alleged misconduct by Lim Kay-Lin Kathlene, noting that her liability was not in issue in this action and that any wrongdoing by her would not absolve the defendants.
facts_to_remove.evidence: This is judgment-stage reasoning.
facts_to_remove.recommended_change: Remove the court's discussion of Ms Lim's alleged misconduct.

facts_to_remove.current_value: The court dismissed the defendants' counterclaims, which were premised on the validity of the September Agreement.
facts_to_remove.evidence: This is the judgment outcome on counterclaims.

facts_to_remove.current_value: In the result, the court ordered that Sean Colville Niven pay APG $400,000 for unpaid share capital and $919,980.97 and A$184,264.10 for payments for his personal use (less amounts recovered from the first defendant), that Wang Zijian pay APG $160,000 for unpaid share capital, and that both defendants jointly and severally account for and, where not justified, repay sums paid into their joint account, with interest at the court rate and costs to APG.
facts_to_remove.evidence: This is the final judgment outcome.
facts_to_remove.recommended_change: Remove the final orders from the pre-dispute factual row.

facts_to_remove.current_value:
The judge held that the appropriation of APG's funds for Sean Colville Niven's personal use constituted a prima facie abuse of funds and applied the principle from Creanovate Pte Ltd v Firstlink Energy Pte Ltd. The court examined each category of payments: American Express card bills, legal fees in Suit No 170 of 2009, payments without supporting documents, family travel expenses, payments to third parties, and the A$86,000 marketing funds. The judge found that many charges on the American Express bills were clearly personal (such as petrol, department stores, supermarkets, restaurants and expensive clothing) and that there were no vouchers or records showing that these expenses were incurred for APG's business. The court placed the burden on Sean Colville Niven, as the director in charge of accounts, to justify the expenses as company expenses and found that he had not done so.

For the legal fees in Suit No 170 of 2009, the court noted that APG was not a party to that suit, that APG paid the defendants' legal fees, and that Sean Colville Niven received settlement money from JL Property without reimbursing APG. The judge found no corroboration of his claim that the non-executive directors had agreed that APG would bear the fees while he kept the settlement proceeds, and held that he had to reimburse APG for those fees, subject to deduction of amounts already recovered from the first defendant law firm.

Regarding payments without supporting documents, the court observed that many payments to Sean Colville Niven were described only as 'furniture', 'artwork', 'loans', 'Niven IRAS' or 'commission' and that he produced no evidence linking them to APG's business or showing entitlement. The judge treated payments described as loans as amounts intended to be repaid and held that he had not justified the commission and incentive payments. For family travel expenses, including trips for his wife, children and domestic helper and a trip to France with his wife, the court found no evidence that APG was obliged to pay these or that they were business-related. For payments to third parties on his behalf, such as telephone charges, school fees and furniture rental, the court found no evidence that these were part of his employment package or incurred for APG's benefit.

On the A$86,000 from Ubertas 350 William Street Pty Ltd, the judge accepted that the funds were paid into his personal account and that he claimed to have spent them on marketing events. However, he produced no receipts or documentation to account for the expenditure and did not call his assistant, whom he said had collected the receipts, to testify. The court held that, as he had received the funds, he had a duty to account for them and, failing proof of application for APG's business, he had to repay the amount.

The court rejected the defendants' reliance on an alleged Account Practice and Oral Agreement authorising the payments. It found that there were no contemporaneous payment vouchers, that the vouchers in evidence were reconstructed by Firstwaters, and that the defendants had not sought discovery of original vouchers. The judge concluded that the Account Practice did not exist and that the allegation that vouchers had been destroyed was baseless. The court also found that the alleged Oral Agreement was unsupported by evidence, inconsistent with clause 5 of the Goh loan agreement and with the formal approach of the non-executive directors, and was denied by the non-executive directors through their solicitors.

On unpaid share capital, the judge found that neither Sean Colville Niven nor Wang Zijian had paid for their original share allotments. The court rejected the argument that the November and December 2009 cheques represented share capital payments, noting that they largely transmitted Ubertas loan funds, were not pleaded as capital payments, and were inconsistent with the September Agreement in which he accepted liability for unpaid capital of $560,000. Applying APG's articles, the court held that he remained liable for unpaid capital on the shares he still held when the call was made (400,000 shares, including 40,000 acquired from the non-executive directors) but not for the 240,000 shares transferred to Lim Kay-Lin Kathlene before the call, as liability for calls attaches to the registered holder at the time of the call.

The judge also considered APG's financial position and held that APG was insolvent or near insolvency by September and October 2010, relying on the Stuart email, Niven's own emails, rent arrears and creditor demands. Applying Chip Thye Enterprises Pte Ltd (in liquidation) v Phay Gi Mo and Progen Engineering, the court held that the September and October Agreements, which wrote off part of his debt, shifted half of his personal debt to APG, and postponed and rearranged unpaid share capital obligations, were not in the interests of APG's creditors and were voidable. The agreements were set aside and did not protect him from liability or support his counterclaim.

The court concluded that Sean Colville Niven was liable to pay APG $400,000 in respect of unpaid share capital, $919,980.97 and A$184,264.10 for payments made for his personal use (less amounts recovered from the first defendant), and, jointly with Wang Zijian, to account for and, where not justified, repay sums paid into their joint account, with interest and costs.
facts_to_remove.evidence: This is entirely judgment reasoning and outcome material.
facts_to_remove.recommended_change: Remove because it is post-dispute judicial reasoning and outcome.

fact_changes.current_value: APG's incorporation records Wang Zijian was allotted 160,000 shares described as fully paid although he later acknowledged that he had never paid for those shares. The September Agreement records his unpaid share capital liability as $160,000 with a ten-year repayment schedule. Bank statements APG funds were transferred into a joint UOB account held by him and Sean Colville Niven, and he had access to that account. From May 2010 he was no longer involved in APG's daily operations, and there is no indication that he personally incurred the specific expenses challenged by APG. These pre-dispute facts support APG's position that he remains responsible for the unpaid capital on his 160,000 shares and that he must, together with Sean Colville Niven, account for APG funds paid into their joint account.
fact_changes.recommended_value: APG's incorporation records show that Wang Zijian was allotted 160,000 shares. The September Agreement records his unpaid share capital liability as $160,000. Bank statements show that APG funds were transferred into a joint UOB account held by him and Sean Colville Niven.
fact_changes.evidence: The current application includes later admissions, legal conclusions, and judgment-stage reasoning about responsibility and lack of personal expense involvement. Those are beyond the temporal wall.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Keep only neutral background facts available before the dispute outcome and remove evaluative conclusions.

fact_changes.current_value:
The judge accepted that there was no allegation that Wang Zijian personally benefited from the specific impugned payments for Sean Colville Niven's expenses. However, the court emphasised that as a director he owed duties to APG and could be responsible where he facilitated or was involved in improper payments. The evidence showed that a joint UOB account was opened in the names of both defendants and that APG funds were regularly transferred into this account, allowing them to bypass the requirement for non-executive directors' signatures on APG cheques. The court held that both defendants were jointly and severally liable to account for sums paid into this joint account and to repay any amounts not shown to have been used for APG's purposes.

Regarding unpaid share capital, the court noted that Wang Zijian admitted he had never paid for his 160,000 shares. The September Agreement recorded his unpaid capital liability as $160,000. The judge held that, subject to the invalidity of the September and October Agreements as defences, he remained liable for the unpaid capital on his shares. Applying the principle that calls on unpaid share capital attach to the registered holder at the time of the call, and noting that he remained the registered holder of 160,000 shares when the call was made in March 2011, the court ordered him to pay $160,000 to APG. The court rejected his argument that responsibility should rest solely with Sean Colville Niven and Lim Kay-Lin Kathlene, distinguishing between his lack of personal benefit from the challenged expenses and his obligations as a director and shareholder in relation to share capital and funds transferred into the joint account.
fact_changes.evidence: This field is entirely judgment reasoning and outcome. It is post-dispute and must not be used for predictive ML features.
fact_changes.recommended_change: Remove the entire judicial reasoning log.
